abstract: Hashing has emerged as a premier technique for efficient similarity retrieval in large-scale multimedia repositories due to its significant savings in storage and rapid query speeds. Recently, supervised multimodal hashing (SMH) has gained traction by incorporating semantic labels to bridge the gap between different data modalities, such as images and text. However, many existing SMH frameworks suffer from high training time complexity—typically quadratic relative to the number of data points—rendering them impractical for truly large-scale applications. In this paper, we introduce a novel approach called Semantic Correlation Maximization (SCM) designed for efficient large-scale data modeling. By seamlessly integrating semantic information without the explicit computation of a massive pairwise similarity matrix, our method achieves a training time complexity that is linear with respect to the dataset size. We propose two learning strategies: one based on orthogonal projections and a sequential learning approach that solves hash functions bit-by-bit via closed-form solutions, eliminating the need for tedious hyper-parameter tuning. Extensive evaluations on the Wiki and NUS-WIDE datasets demonstrate that SCM consistently outperforms state-of-the-art multimodal hashing methods, delivering superior retrieval accuracy while maintaining exceptional scalability.
